metadata.subject: Physical and Health Education
metadata.description: Outdoor Education 11 develops students' competence, confidence, and deep engagement with outdoor environments through wilderness skills, risk management, and environmental stewardship. Students explore camping, orienteering, hiking, backcountry travel, and wilderness survival fundamentals while developing self-reliance, problem-solving, and decision-making in outdoor contexts. The course emphasizes First Peoples land-based knowledge, relationships with the natural world, and sustainable outdoor practices. Students engage with outdoor recreation safely and responsibly, understanding risks and environmental impacts of their activities.
metadata.general_description: A practical experiential course developing wilderness skills, outdoor leadership, environmental stewardship, and safe, sustainable engagement with BC's diverse outdoor environments.

big_ideas.statement: Competence and safety in outdoor environments develop through systematic skill building, knowledge, and reflective practice.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students progressively develop wilderness skills — navigation, camp craft, weather assessment, risk management — that build confidence and enable safe outdoor participation.

big_ideas.statement: Connection to place and deep environmental awareness emerge through sustained outdoor experience and First Peoples perspectives.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students develop meaningful relationships with natural environments through time spent outdoors, learning to observe ecological patterns, understand their interdependence with nature, and appreciate First Peoples land stewardship wisdom.

big_ideas.statement: Risk assessment and management are essential to responsible outdoor decision-making and safe recreation.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students learn to identify hazards, assess risk levels, apply appropriate risk mitigation strategies, and make informed decisions about outdoor activities and conditions.

big_ideas.statement: Environmental stewardship and Leave No Trace principles guide ethical outdoor recreation and land care.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students understand their responsibility to minimize environmental impact, practice sustainable outdoor recreation, and contribute to conservation of natural spaces.

big_ideas.statement: Outdoor experiences foster resilience, self-reliance, and personal growth through challenge and reflection.
big_ideas.elaboration: Students develop confidence, problem-solving skills, and self-awareness through outdoor challenges, building capacity to manage adversity and pursue lifelong outdoor engagement.

categories.name: Wilderness Skills and Competence
categories.raw: Developing practical competence in camping, navigation, and wilderness survival fundamentals.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate camping and shelter skills; apply navigation methods including map and compass; understand wilderness survival fundamentals; apply appropriate equipment use and care; develop self-reliance in outdoor contexts.
i_can_statements: I can select, set up, and maintain camp sites appropriately — including shelter, cooking area, and waste management.
I can navigate using maps, compass, and landscape features with increasing independence and confidence.
I can apply wilderness survival fundamentals — water sourcing, fire building, shelter creation, and signaling — in appropriate contexts.
I can select, maintain, and repair outdoor equipment — tents, backpacks, sleeping systems, and clothing layers.
I can make safe decisions about wilderness travel, recognizing my skill level and appropriate challenge levels.

categories.name: Risk Assessment and Outdoor Decision-Making
categories.raw: Understanding hazards, assessing risk, and making informed decisions in outdoor contexts.

competencies.raw: Identify outdoor hazards and assess risk levels; apply risk mitigation strategies; make informed decisions about outdoor activities and conditions; respond to emergencies appropriately.
i_can_statements: I can identify common outdoor hazards — weather, terrain, wildlife, water, and human factors — and assess their risk levels.
I can apply appropriate risk management strategies: avoiding unnecessary risks, mitigating unavoidable risks, and accepting calculated risks.
I can make informed decisions about whether conditions are suitable for planned activities, based on weather, skill level, and hazard assessment.
I can recognize signs of environmental distress — hypothermia, dehydration, heat exhaustion — and respond with appropriate first aid.
I can communicate risks and decisions clearly with group members and take leadership in group safety.

categories.name: Environmental Stewardship and Outdoor Ethics
categories.raw: Understanding environmental responsibility, sustainability, and First Peoples land relationships.

competencies.raw: Apply Leave No Trace principles; understand impacts of outdoor recreation; explore First Peoples land stewardship practices; engage with conservation and sustainability; demonstrate ethical outdoor behavior.
i_can_statements: I can apply Leave No Trace principles across all outdoor activities — minimizing campfire impacts, managing waste, respecting wildlife, and protecting water sources.
I can explain how outdoor recreation impacts ecosystems and landscapes, and identify strategies to reduce negative impacts.
I can explore and respect First Peoples perspectives on land stewardship, reciprocal relationships with nature, and sustainable resource use.
I can engage with local conservation efforts, understand the importance of protected areas, and support environmental sustainability.
I can model and advocate for ethical outdoor behavior, including respect for private property, responsible access, and inclusive recreation.

categories.name: Outdoor Leadership and Resilience
categories.raw: Developing leadership capacity, resilience, and personal growth through outdoor experiences.

competencies.raw: Lead outdoor activities and groups; support peers in outdoor learning; develop resilience and confidence through outdoor challenge; reflect on personal growth and outdoor experiences.
i_can_statements: I can plan and lead outdoor activities and trips, managing logistics, safety, and group dynamics.
I can support and mentor peers in developing outdoor skills and confidence, providing encouragement and constructive feedback.
I can persevere through outdoor challenges, adapt to changing conditions, and develop problem-solving approaches.
I can articulate how outdoor experiences have built my confidence, resilience, and self-understanding.
I can identify outdoor careers and pathways, and consider how outdoor education and skills might shape my future.

areas.name: Camping and Camp Craft
areas.raw: Practical skills for comfortable and sustainable camping in varied environments.
topics: Site selection and assessment: choosing appropriate camping locations, evaluating terrain and hazards, and minimizing environmental impact
Shelter systems: understanding tents, tarps, and other shelter types — setup, ventilation, weatherproofing, and maintenance
Fire building and safety: fire types, fuel selection, fire safety, and leave-no-trace fire practices
Cooking and nutrition in the outdoors: field cooking equipment, food selection, meal planning, and waste management
Water management: safe water sourcing, treatment methods, and hydration in wilderness contexts
Sanitation and hygiene: appropriate toilet facilities, personal hygiene, and waste management in pristine areas

areas.name: Navigation and Route Planning
areas.raw: Developing competence in finding and following routes through landscape.
topics: Map reading: understanding map symbols, contours, scale, and terrain representation
Compass navigation: orienting maps, taking and following bearings, triangulation, and declination
Landscape navigation: using terrain features, vegetation patterns, and natural landmarks for navigation
Route planning: assessing distance, elevation gain, terrain difficulty, and logistics for multi-day trips
GPS and technology: using GPS devices and mapping applications as navigational supplements
Navigation in poor visibility: techniques for navigating when visibility is limited by weather or darkness

areas.name: Wilderness Survival and Emergency Response
areas.raw: Fundamental knowledge and skills for managing emergencies and survival situations.
topics: Survival priorities: understanding the hierarchy of survival needs and immediate response to emergencies
Shelter creation: building emergency shelters from natural materials and available resources
Fire building without tools: fire-building fundamentals and methods for creating fire in wilderness contexts
Water and food sourcing: identifying safe water sources, water treatment, and edible plants and animals
Signaling and rescue: methods for signaling rescuers, leaving information, and increasing visibility
Hypothermia and heat management: preventing and responding to temperature-related emergencies

areas.name: Outdoor Hazards and Risk Management
areas.raw: Understanding environmental hazards and making safe decisions in outdoor contexts.
topics: Weather assessment: reading weather patterns, forecasting, and adapting to changing weather
Terrain hazards: understanding risks of avalanche terrain, unstable ground, water hazards, and rock fall
Wildlife awareness: understanding animal behavior, appropriate responses, and wildlife safety protocols
Human hazards: managing group dynamics, preventing injuries, and maintaining mental and physical health
Risk assessment frameworks: using systematic approaches to identify and evaluate risks
Emergency response: wilderness first aid, evacuation procedures, and communication in remote areas

areas.name: First Peoples Land Knowledge and Environmental Stewardship
areas.raw: Understanding Indigenous land relationships, ecological knowledge, and sustainable practices.
topics: First Peoples perspectives: learning about Indigenous relationships with land, traditional resource use, and cultural practices
Ecological knowledge: understanding local ecosystems, plants, animals, and seasonal patterns through Indigenous perspectives
Sustainable resource practices: learning traditional harvesting practices, reciprocal relationships, and conservation ethics
Land and health connections: understanding how land connection supports physical, mental, and spiritual well-being
Protected areas and conservation: understanding reserves, conservation areas, and their role in preserving lands and supporting Indigenous rights
Colonization impacts: understanding how colonization has affected Indigenous relationships with land and current environmental conditions

areas.name: Outdoor Recreation and Lifestyle
areas.raw: Engaging with diverse outdoor activities and developing lifelong outdoor participation.
topics: Hiking and backpacking: day hikes and multi-day trips in varied terrain and seasons
Paddling: kayaking, canoeing, and water travel fundamentals and environmental practices
Rock climbing and scrambling: rock movement fundamentals, safety systems, and risk management
Winter activities: snowshoeing, backcountry skiing, and winter-specific wilderness skills
Seasonal outdoor pursuits: adapting outdoor engagement to seasons and weather conditions
Outdoor recreation access and equity: understanding barriers to outdoor participation and advocating for inclusive access
